features.name: Resource Hierarchy Governance
features.description: Enforces resource hierarchy access policies from Organization to Project, inheriting permissions downwards.

features.name: Least Privilege Role Controls
features.description: Assigns fine-grained, predefined roles to user groups, preventing over-privileged credentials.

features.name: Zero-Trust Remote Access
features.description: Secures web applications and administrative consoles without VPNs using Identity-Aware Proxy.

iamRoles.description: Full access to create, modify, and delete IAM policies across resources.
Permissions to create and manage projects within folders or organizations.

cliExamples.description: Grant a service account the Storage Object Viewer role
List all IAM role bindings configured on a project

design: Identity and Access Management (IAM) defines who (identity) has what access (role) on which resource.
Resource hierarchy (Organization -> Folders -> Projects -> Resources) enforces inherited policy controls.
Permissions inherit downwards; policy restrictions cannot deny permissions granted at higher resource levels.
Assign roles to Google Groups instead of individual users to simplify access administration.
Service Accounts are identities for applications; they do not have passwords and use public/private key pairs.
User-managed service account keys are a security risk; rotate them regularly or use Workload Identity.
IAM Conditions grant access to resources only when conditions match (e.g., specific date, IP, resource naming).
Directory Sync syncs identities from external providers (Active Directory, Azure AD) into Cloud Identity.
security: Enforce least privilege by avoiding primitive roles (Owner, Editor, Viewer) in production environments.
Enable Service Account Impersonation, letting developers generate short-lived credentials instead of downloading JSON keys.
Workload Identity Federation allows external workloads (AWS, Azure, GitHub Actions) to authenticate without static keys.
Use Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) to enforce access controls on administrative web apps.
Audit administrative access and role assignments by regularly scanning Cloud Audit Logs.
Enable Organization Policies to block the creation of external service account keys entirely.
Use IAM Recommender to identify over-privileged users and automatically strip unused permissions.
Configure Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) in Cloud Identity for all enterprise users.
reliability: IAM authorization checks are globally distributed and cached at edge points, ensuring sub-millisecond evaluation.
Identity metadata is replicated globally across Google Cloud datacenters for high availability.
If external identity syncing fails, cached credentials in Cloud Identity allow continued operation.
Design critical service accounts with backup permissions to ensure automation pipelines do not fail.
SSO (Single Sign-On) configuration supports automated failover across redundant identity endpoints.
IAM modifications propagate globally within seconds, minimizing access delay.
System authentication errors and access denials are logged to Cloud Logging automatically.
Maintain IAM policy configurations as Code (IaC) in git to enable quick restore from policy errors.
cost: IAM, Cloud Identity Free, and basic Resource Manager services are provided at no cost.
Syncing external identities and using basic IAM authorization check APIs is free.
Audit log storage is billed based on retention; route logs to Cloud Storage Archive to save cost.
Use IAM Recommender suggestions to downsize roles, reducing operational risk and management audit fees.
Consolidate multiple projects into shared folders to simplify policy management, reducing admin overhead.
Automate the identification and deletion of inactive service accounts to save audit processing costs.
Cloud Identity Premium is billed per user; allocate premium licenses only to users requiring advanced device controls.
Minimize cross-project service account access requests to reduce audit log size and related storage billing.
